items.title: Clinical Diagnosis of Behçet Syndrome
items.content: Behçet syndrome is diagnosed based on clinical features, as there are no specific laboratory or imaging tests for diagnosis. The International Study Group (ISG) criteria are most commonly used, requiring recurrent oral ulcers and two additional manifestations such as genital ulcers, skin lesions, eye lesions, or a positive pathergy test.
keyPoints: Diagnosis is clinical with no specific lab/imaging features
International Study Group (ISG) criteria sensitivity ~95%, specificity ~96%
Criteria: recurrent oral ulcers + 2 of genital ulcers, skin lesions, eye lesions, or positive pathergy test
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · Behçet Syndrome | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: HLA B*51 Association in Behçet Syndrome
items.content: Up to 50–60% of patients with Behçet syndrome may be positive for HLA B*51, but it is not used diagnostically due to its presence in ~20% of the normal population.
keyPoints: HLA B*51 positivity in 50-60% of patients
Not diagnostic as it's found in ~20% of normal population
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · Behçet Syndrome | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Understanding Behçet Syndrome Pathogenesis and Clinical Presentation
items.content: Behçet syndrome's pathogenesis involves genetic predisposition, inflammation, and immune system involvement. It differs from autoimmune diseases due to the absence of autoantibodies and other typical features but shares some characteristics with autoinflammatory conditions. Clinically, mucocutaneous symptoms are prominent, with oral ulcers being nearly universal and genital ulcers more specific.
keyPoints: Genetic predisposition and immune involvement without typical autoimmune markers
Neutrophil hyperreactivity; unclear if primary or secondary to cytokine activation
Different disease clusters may have distinct pathogenesis (e.g., acne with arthritis)
Oral ulcers are common, usually multiple, last ~10 days, recur unless treated
Genital ulcers are specific, larger, deeper, and scar-forming
Acne-like lesions can appear at uncommon sites like lower extremities
Superficial thrombophlebitis in men may indicate deep-vein thrombosis risk
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Clinical Red Flags and Management of Behçet Syndrome
items.content: Key clinical features include oral ulcers as a common initial symptom, genital ulcers that are specific to the syndrome, and skin manifestations like acne-like lesions. The pathergy reaction is a notable hyperreactivity to trauma. Superficial thrombophlebitis in men warrants further vascular evaluation due to associated risks.
keyPoints: Oral ulcers: common initial symptom, usually multiple, last ~10 days
Genital ulcers: specific, larger, deeper, scar-forming
Acne-like lesions can appear at uncommon sites like lower extremities
Pathergy reaction: papule or pustule forms 24–48 hours after trauma
Superficial thrombophlebitis in men may indicate deep-vein thrombosis risk
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: Which combination of symptoms is required for a diagnosis of Behçet syndrome according to the International Study Group (ISG) criteria?
options: Recurrent oral ulcers and one other clinical manifestation
Recurrent genital ulcers, skin lesions, eye lesions, or a positive pathergy test
Recurrent oral ulcers plus two of the following: recurrent genital ulcers, skin lesions, eye lesions, or a positive pathergy test
HLA B*51 positivity and recurrent oral ulcers
items.explanation: The ISG criteria require recurrent oral ulcers plus two other manifestations for diagnosis.
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · Behçet Syndrome | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT used as a diagnostic test for Behçet syndrome?
items.explanation: HLA B*51 is not used as a diagnostic test due to its presence in around 20% of the normal population.
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · Behçet Syndrome | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the specificity of the International Study Group (ISG) criteria for diagnosing Behçet syndrome?
items.explanation: The ISG criteria have a specificity of ~96%.
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · Behçet Syndrome | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which feature is NOT typically associated with Behçet syndrome?
items.explanation: Behçet syndrome is not typically associated with autoantibodies.
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: What suggests that decreased oral health may be linked to Behçet syndrome severity?
options: Genital ulcers are more common than oral ulcers.
Dental and periodontal therapies have beneficial effects on symptoms.
Acne-like lesions occur only at usual acne sites.
items.explanation: Beneficial effects of dental and periodontal therapies suggest that decreased oral health is associated with disease severity.
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.question: Which statement about the pathogenesis of Behçet syndrome is true?
options: There are mutations associated with it.
Neutrophil hyperreactivity may be secondary to cytokine-directed activation.
items.explanation: Neutrophil hyperreactivity in Behçet syndrome may be secondary to cytokine-directed activation.
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: Laboratory, imaging, or histologic features are specific for diagnosing Behçet syndrome.
items.explanation: There are no specific laboratory, imaging, or histologic features used to diagnose Behçet syndrome.
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · Behçet Syndrome | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: The diagnosis of Behçet syndrome is based solely on the presence of recurrent oral ulcers.
items.explanation: Diagnosis requires recurrent oral ulcers plus two other clinical manifestations.
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · Behçet Syndrome | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Behçet syndrome is typically associated with serosal involvement.
items.explanation: Behçet syndrome is not typically associated with serosal involvement.
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.statement: Genital ulcers in Behçet syndrome tend to form scars.
items.explanation: Genital ulcers are larger, deeper, take longer to heal than oral ulcers and tend to form scars.
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · PATHOGENESIS | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.assertion: The diagnosis of Behçet syndrome can be made based on a combination of clinical features.
items.reason: There are no specific laboratory, imaging, or histologic features for diagnosing Behçet syndrome.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the lack of specific tests necessitates reliance on clinical features.
items.reference: Beh et Syndrome · Behçet Syndrome | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Behçet syndrome is not typically associated with autoantibodies.
items.reason: Unlike other autoimmune diseases, Behçet syndrome does not have typical features such as autoantibodies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why Behçet syndrome is not typically associated with autoantibodies.
